meta.focus: Forming the て-form and た-form of all verb types; chaining sequential actions; making polite requests with てください; negative requests with ないでください; firm commands with なさい

sections.title: て-form — Connecting, Requesting & Commanding
sections.summary: The て-form (te-form) is one of the most important forms in Japanese. It connects two actions in sequence, forms polite requests, and underlies many patterns you'll use every day.
sections.whyItMatters: With only ます and ました, you can describe only one action per sentence. The て-form unlocks chaining (行って来ました — went and came back), polite requests (書いてください — please write), negative requests (行かないでください — please don't go), and firm commands (書きなさい — write!). These patterns appear in every conversation from this point forward.
youWillLearn: Ichidan て-form: drop る, add て
Godan て-form: euphonic change rules by verb ending (including the 行く exception!)
Irregular て-form: 来る → 来て (きて), する → して
た-form: identical sound-change rules to て-form, ending in た/だ instead of て/で
Using て to connect sequential actions (AてB = 'did A, then B')
Polite requests: [て-form] + ください — 'please do'
Negative requests: [negative stem] + ないでください — 'please don't'
Firm commands: [ます-stem] + なさい — used by teachers and parents

sections.meaning: Ichidan て-form: drop る and add て
sections.explanation: Ichidan (RU-verb) て-form follows the same pattern as all other ichidan conjugations: simply remove the final る from the dictionary form, then attach て. If you know the ichidan ます stem, you already know the て stem — they are identical.
notes: The rule: [stem] + て — where the stem is the dictionary form minus る.
いる → い + て = いて ('being here, and then...')
The て-form alone is incomplete — it connects to a following action or clause.
Ichidan verbs always produce a て-form ending in て (not で). Godan verbs may end in either て or で depending on their final syllable.

examples.en: to be here → being here (and then...)
examples.breakdown: Ichidan: drop る from いる → い + て = いて

examples.en: My friend is here, and then... (connects to next clause)
examples.breakdown: いる → いて (ichidan). て connects to what happens next. Particles: が marks the subject (who is here); に marks the location of existence with いる (G5).

sections.meaning: Godan て-form: euphonic change rules — the ending determines the て-form
sections.explanation: Godan (U-verb) て-forms are more complex than ichidan. The final syllable of the dictionary form determines which て-form rule applies. These are called 'euphonic changes' — the sounds shift to make pronunciation easier and more natural.
notes: く → いて: 書く → 書いて (write). BUT: 行く → 行って is a FAMOUS EXCEPTION — not 行いて!
ぐ → いで: 泳ぐ → 泳いで (swim).
む / ぬ / ぶ → んで: 飲む → 飲んで (drink); 死ぬ → 死んで (die); 遊ぶ → 遊んで (play).
つ / る / う → って: 持つ → 持って (hold); 分かる → 分かって (understand); 言う → 言って (say).
す → して: 話す → 話して (speak).
Memory tip: 行く is the only く-verb that breaks the く→いて rule. Memorise it separately.

examples.en: to go → going (and then...) — famous irregular て-form!
examples.breakdown: 行く: normally く→いて, but 行く is a special exception → 行って (not 行いて)

examples.en: to understand → understanding (and then...)
examples.breakdown: 分かる is godan (か = あ-row before る). Godan る → って: 分かって

examples.en: I went to the station and then came (back).
examples.breakdown: 行く → 行って (godan て exception); connects to 来ました. Particles: に marks 駅 as the destination (G5).

sections.meaning: Irregular て-form: する → して, 来る → 来て (きて)
sections.explanation: Japanese has two fully irregular verbs: する (to do) and 来る (to come). Their て-forms cannot be derived from any rule — they must be memorised. Fortunately, you already know them: する → して is the same stem used in します, and 来る → 来て (きて) has the same reading as きます. The kanji 来 keeps its written form in both.
notes: する → して. (Not すて, not すって.) The reading changes completely: す → し.
来る → 来て (read as きて). The kanji 来 stays, but the reading shifts from く to き.
Both forms are identical to the ます-stem: する → し (します / して), 来る → き (きます / 来て).
Memory hook: if you know します and きます, you already know して and 来て — same stem, different ending.
These two verbs are also irregular in their negative stem: する → し + ないでください; 来る → こ + ないでください (reading changes again! こ, not き).
Negative reminder: する → しない, 来る → こない — completely different stems from the て-form.
What about ある? ある's て-form is actually REGULAR (godan る → あって). Its irregularity is only in the negative: ある → ない (not あらない). So ある follows the normal て-form rules — only its negative is special.

examples.en: to do → doing (and then...)
examples.breakdown: する is fully irregular. て-form = して. (ます-stem し + て = して)

parts.gloss: dictionary form (to come) — read くる

examples.en: to come → coming (and then...)
examples.breakdown: 来る is fully irregular. て-form = 来て (きて). The kanji stays but the reading changes: く→き.

parts.gloss: polite past — sets tense for both

examples.en: I studied and then came to the station.
examples.breakdown: する → して (irregular); connects to 来ました (also irregular). Both irregulars in one sentence! Particle: に marks 駅 as the destination (G5).

sections.meaning: AてB — 'did A, and then B' (sequential actions)
sections.explanation: The て-form's core use is connecting two actions by the same subject in sequence. The first verb takes the て-form, and the second verb carries the tense for the whole sentence. Both actions happened (or will happen) in order.
notes: Tense is determined only by the FINAL verb. て-form itself is tenseless.
駅へ行って、コンビニに来ました = Went to the station, then came to the convenience store (both past).

In basic AてB sequences, the subject is usually the same person performing both actions. However, different subjects are possible when describing a sequence of events (うちにいて、友だちが来ました — I was at home, and my friend came).
You can chain more than two actions: AてBてC = 'did A, then B, then C'.
て-form also underlies other patterns (～てください, ～ています) taught in later lessons.

parts.gloss: direction — emphasises heading TOWARD the station (G5)
destination — emphasises ARRIVING at the store (G5)
polite past — sets tense for both actions

examples.en: I went to the station and then came to the convenience store.
examples.breakdown: 行く→行って (て-form, exception); 来ました sets past tense for both actions. Particles: へ marks the station as a direction (heading toward it), while に marks the convenience store as a destination (arriving there). Both work with movement verbs, but へ emphasises the journey and に emphasises the arrival point (G5). You could swap them — 駅に行って、コンビニへ来ました would also be correct.

parts.gloss: existence location — WHERE I was (に marks location with いる, G5)
subject marker — marks WHO came (new information, G3)

examples.en: I was at home, and then my friend came.
examples.breakdown: いる→いて (ichidan); connects to 来ました. Particles: に marks うち as the location of existence — with いる, に always shows WHERE someone is (G5). This is different from the destination に in the previous example! が marks 友だち as the subject — it introduces new information (WHO came). If it were 友だちは来ました, the は would signal 'as for my friend...' (topic, with possible contrast, G3).

parts.gloss: polite present/future — sets tense for both

examples.en: I will go to the station and come again.
examples.breakdown: て-form is tenseless; 来ます (present/future) sets tense for both actions. Particles: に marks 駅 as the destination. Here に could be replaced with へ (駅へ行って) — both are correct for movement verbs (G5).

sections.meaning: てください — polite request: 'please do [verb]'
sections.explanation: Attach ください directly to the て-form to make a polite request. This is one of the highest-frequency patterns in everyday Japanese — used for directions, instructions, shopping, and any situation where you want someone to do something.
notes: Formation: [て-form] + ください (no space, no particle between them).
書いてください — Please write. 来てください — Please come. 話してください — Please speak.
Negative request (please don't): [negative ない-stem] + ないでください.
行かないでください — Please don't go. (行く: godan → あ-shift → 行か + ないでください)
話さないでください — Please don't speak. (話す: godan → あ-shift → 話さ + ないでください)
ないでください attaches to the plain negative stem (あ-column for godan, drop る for ichidan), NOT to the て-form.
てください is polite but direct. Adding お願いします at the end makes it warmer: 来てください、お願いします.

examples.en: Please come here.
examples.breakdown: 来る → 来て (irregular). 来て + ください = polite request. Particles: に marks ここ as the destination (G5).

examples.en: Please write.
examples.breakdown: 書く (godan く) → 書いて + ください = 書いてください.

parts.gloss: go(negative stem: く→か, あ-row shift)

examples.en: Please don't go to the convenience store.
examples.breakdown: Negative request: 行く → あ-row shift く→か → 行か + ないでください. See the あ-row shift table below for all godan endings. Particles: に marks コンビニ as the destination (G5).

sections.title: The あ-Row Shift — Negative Stem Map
sections.description: To form ないでください (please don't), you need the negative stem. For godan verbs, this means shifting the final syllable to the あ-row — just as the ます stem uses the い-row. Here is every godan ending and its あ-row shift. Pay special attention to the one exception.

rows.label: く
cells: 書く → 書かないでください

rows.label: ぐ
cells: およぐ → およがないでください

rows.label: す
cells: はなす → はなさないでください

rows.label: つ
cells: もつ → もたないでください

rows.label: ぬ
cells: しぬ → しなないでください

rows.label: ぶ
cells: よぶ → よばないでください

rows.label: む
cells: のむ → のまないでください

rows.label: る (godan)
cells: 分かる → 分からないでください
rows.meaning: Please don't understand (rare, but shows the pattern)

rows.label: う ⚠️
cells: かう → かわないでください
rows.meaning: EXCEPTION: please don't buy — う shifts to わ, not あ

notes: Same logic as the い-row shift (ます stem, G6) — find the ending on the hiragana chart and move it to the target row. For ない, the target is the あ-row.
⚠️ THE ONE EXCEPTION: verbs ending in う shift to わ, NOT あ. This is a fixed historical rule: かう (buy) → かわない, いう (say) → いわない, あう (meet) → あわない. If you wrote かあない, it would be wrong — always use わ.
Why う → わ? In Old Japanese, the あ-row character for the う-column was わ. Modern Japanese preserved this single irregularity. Every other ending shifts exactly as the hiragana chart predicts.
For ichidan verbs, the negative stem is simpler: just drop る. 食べる → 食べ + ないでください. いる → い + ないでください.
For irregular verbs: する → し + ないでください. 来る → こ + ないでください (reading changes again!). ある → negative is simply ない (irregular — not あらない).

sections.meaning: ～なさい — firm polite command using the ます-stem
sections.explanation: なさい attaches to the verb ます-stem (the same stem used to form ます). It is firmer and more directive than てください, but gentler than blunt plain commands. Teachers use it with students, parents with children — it carries authority without rudeness.
notes: Attaches to the ます-stem (NOT the て-form): 食べる → 食べ + なさい = 食べなさい.
Ichidan: 食べる → 食べ + なさい = 食べなさい (eat!).
Godan: 書く → 書き + なさい = 書きなさい (write!); 話す → 話し + なさい = 話しなさい (speak!).
Irregular: する → し + なさい = しなさい (do it!); 来る → き + なさい = きなさい (come here!).
No standard negative なさい form. Use ないでください for 'please don't'.
Context: teachers giving instructions, parents directing children. NOT used between peers or from junior to senior — that would be very rude.
Contrast: てください = polite request (anyone can use). なさい = firm instruction (authority → subordinate).

examples.en: Eat! (parent to child)
examples.breakdown: 食べる (ichidan): drop る → 食べ + なさい = 食べなさい.

examples.en: Write! (teacher to student)
examples.breakdown: 書く (godan く): ます-stem = 書き + なさい = 書きなさい.

examples.en: Come here! (teacher calling a student)
examples.breakdown: 来る (irregular): ます-stem = き + なさい = きなさい.

sections.title: て-form and た-form Conjugation Table
sections.description: て-form and た-form follow identical sound-change rules — only the final sound differs (て/で vs た/だ). Note the famous 行く exception.

rows.label: Ichidan
cells: Drop る, add て / た

rows.label: Godan く (EXCEPTION!)
cells: く → って / った (行く only — not 行いて!)

notes: 行く is the only common N5 verb with a く ending that breaks the く→いて rule. All other く verbs follow く→いて / く→いた.
Godan る verbs (like 分かる where the vowel before る is not い/え) always follow る→って/った.
来る is fully irregular — て-form is 来て (きて), た-form is 来た (きた).
ある て-form: あって, た-form: あった — regular godan る→って/った rule (ある's irregularity is its negative form ない, not its て/た forms).
The た-form (plain past) is covered in depth in G9. Here, learn the sound-change rules — they are identical to て-form.

sections.title: て-form in Sequential, Request, and Command Contexts

examples.context: Two past actions in sequence — に marks both destinations

parts.gloss: yesterday (relative time — no particle needed, G4)
polite past — sets tense for both

examples.en: Yesterday I went to the station and then came to the convenience store.
examples.note: 行く→行って (exception!). ました on the final verb sets the past tense for both actions. Both に particles mark destinations (G5). きのう takes no particle because it is a relative time word (G4).

examples.context: Two future actions planned in sequence — に for both destinations

parts.gloss: next week (relative time — no particle, G4)

examples.en: Next week I will go to the convenience store and then come to the supermarket.
examples.note: 来ます (present/future) shows this hasn't happened yet. The て-form itself has no tense. Particles: both に mark destinations (G5). 来週 is relative time, so no particle (G4).

examples.context: Ichidan て-form — に marks existence location (not destination!)

parts.gloss: existence location — WHERE I am (に + いる, G5)

examples.en: Being at home, and coming every day.
examples.note: いる→いて (ichidan): drop る, add て. Particles: に here marks existence location (WHERE I am, with いる) — different from the destination に in the examples above (G5). Same particle, different role!

examples.context: Polite request with てください — に marks destination
examples.en: Please come here.
examples.note: て-form + ください = polite request. 来る → 来て (irregular te-form). The most common way to make a polite request.

examples.context: Negative request with ないでください — あ-row shift for godan

parts.gloss: go(negative stem: く→か, あ-row shift)

examples.en: Please don't go to the convenience store.
examples.note: Negative request: 行く → あ-row shift (く→か) → 行か + ないでください. Attaches to the negative stem, NOT the て-form. See the あ-row shift table for all godan endings — and watch out for う→わ!

examples.context: Firm command with なさい (teacher to student)
examples.en: Write! (firm instruction)
examples.note: なさい uses the ます-stem: 書く → 書き + なさい = 書きなさい. Firmer than てください — used by teachers and parents giving instructions.

sections.title: かいわ 1: きのう どこに 行きましたか（Where Did You Go Yesterday?）
sections.context: Yamakawa tells Rikizo about his outing yesterday, using て-form to chain the actions.

lines.jp: やまかわさん、きのう どこに 行きましたか。
lines.en: Yamakawa-san, where did you go yesterday?

lines.jp: 駅に 行って、コンビニに 来ました。
lines.en: I went to the station and then came to the convenience store.

lines.jp: そうですか。バスで 行きましたか。
lines.en: Is that so. Did you go by bus?

lines.jp: はい、バスで 行って、駅に 来ました。
lines.en: Yes, I went by bus and came to the station.

lines.jp: 友だちも 来ましたか。
はい、友だちが 来て、コンビニに 行きました。とても いいです。
lines.en: Yes, my friend came and then we went to the convenience store. It's really great.

sections.title: かいわ 2: 先生のしじ（Teacher's Instructions）
sections.context: The teacher uses てください requests to guide Rikizo through classroom tasks, and demonstrates ないでください for a negative instruction.

lines.jp: りきぞさん、ここに 来てください。
きいてください。駅に 行って、コンビニに 来ました。
lines.en: Please listen. I went to the station and then came to the convenience store.

lines.jp: 分かりました。かきます。
それから、友だちと はなしてください。コンビニには 行かないでください。
lines.en: After that, please speak with your friend. Please do not go to the convenience store.

lines.jp: 分かりました。行きません。

sections.title: て-form Practice
sections.instructions: Choose the correct て-form, request form, or correctly formed sentence.

items.q: What is the て-form of「行く」?
items.explanation: 行く is the famous exception to the godan く→いて rule. Instead of 行いて, the correct て-form is 行って. This must be memorised — it is one of the most common verbs in Japanese.

items.q: What is the て-form of「いる」(to be/exist)?
items.explanation: いる is ichidan (い is in the い-row before る). Ichidan て-form: drop る, add て → い + て = いて.

items.q: What is the て-form of「来る」?
items.explanation: 来る is an irregular verb. Its て-form is 来て (reading: きて). This cannot be derived from a rule — it must be memorised.

items.q: Which sentence correctly means 'I went to the station and then came to the store'?
choices: 駅に 行って、店に 来ました。
駅に 行きました、店に 来ました。
駅に 行いて、店に 来ました。
駅に 行って、店に 来て。
items.answer: 駅に 行って、店に 来ました。
items.explanation: て-form connects sequential actions: 行って (go + connector). The final verb 来ました carries the past tense for both. 行いて is wrong (行く exception). Ending with 来て would leave the sentence unfinished.

items.q: What is the て-form of「分かる」(to understand)?
items.explanation: 分かる is godan (か = あ-row before る → る-trap). Godan る → って: 分かる → 分かって. (This follows the る/つ/う → って rule, NOT the く→いて rule.)

items.q: In the sentence「コンビニに 行って、友だちが 来ます。」what tense does the whole sentence describe?
choices: The て-form makes the first action past and the second future
items.explanation: The て-form has no tense of its own. Tense is determined entirely by the final verb: 来ます is polite present/future, so the whole sequence (going to the convenience store, then friend coming) describes a planned future sequence.
てください = [て-form] + ください. 話す (godan す) → 話して + ください = 話してください. 話しますください is wrong — you cannot attach ください to the ます form. 話さないでください means 'please don't speak' — the opposite request.

items.q: Which sentence correctly means 'Please don't go to the convenience store'?
choices: コンビニに 行かないでください。
コンビニに 行ってください。
コンビニに 行かないください。
コンビニに 行きません。
items.answer: コンビニに 行かないでください。
items.explanation: Negative request = [negative ない-stem] + ないでください. 行く (godan): shift く→か (あ-column) → 行か + ないでください. 行ってください means 'please go' — the opposite. 行かないください is missing the で and is ungrammatical. 行きません is a simple negative statement, not a request.
